metadata.title: Spec Before You Build: The Five Components
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 BOTSPEAK PROMPT ADAPTATION — E2/5 — SPECIFICATION RIGOR (16 pts)
metadata.playlist: The Botspeak Prompt Adaptation — INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI
metadata.source: INFO 7375 (Computational Skepticism for AI) — Botspeak Prompt Adaptation (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. Episode two of five: the five-component specification. This component is sixteen points and most students lose them the same way — by writing the spec after the prompt, or by collapsing all five components into a single intent statement that does not actually constrain anything. The skill Botspeak is testing here is not whether you can name the five components. It is whether you know what a correct prompt looks like before you write it. A spec retrofitted from a finished prompt reads as one. Write it first. Let's go.

props.segment: Spec Before You Build: The Five Components
props.command: Write the five-component specification for your adapted prompt — Intent, Constraints, Success Criteria, Exclusions, Output Format — before you write the prompt.
props.runningText: the spec is the work — the prompt is just the document that records it…
output: Intent — the goal the task serves, not the task itself
Constraints, Success Criteria, Exclusions, Output Format — all five present

beats.narration_text: Botspeak's five-component specification is: Intent, Constraints, Success Criteria, Exclusions, and Output Format. All five must be present. None can be collapsed into another. The list is not glamorous — it is infrastructure. Infrastructure that holds up the prompt the way a foundation holds up a building. If you are missing one component, the model will make up what you did not specify, and it will make it up plausibly, which makes it hard to name why the output is wrong. Each component closes a specific gap. Here is what each one closes.

shot.description: Five-component vertical stack diagram. Each component is a labeled row: (1) INTENT — cream card, ink label, caption 'The goal the task serves — not the task itself'. (2) CONSTRAINTS — same, caption 'What the output must fit inside'. (3) SUCCESS CRITERIA — terracotta accent, caption 'Testable: a pass/fail you can actually check'. (4) EXCLUSIONS — same, caption 'Your role's typical failure modes — not generic'. (5) OUTPUT FORMAT — same, caption 'The shape you need back'. Rows stacked vertically with light dividers, filling the canvas vertically. Claude palette (#FAF9F5 / #3D3929 / #D97757). Title: 'The Five-Component Specification' in EB Garamond at top.

beats.narration_text: Intent is not the task. The task is 'summarize this report.' The intent is 'give the editorial desk five reportable-angle bullets they can argue against at the nine AM standup to pick the two stories they will commission this week.' Those are not the same thing, and the model cannot know which one you mean if you do not say it. The intent statement tells the model — and you — what counts as success. Write it in one sentence. If you need more than one sentence, you have not yet decided what the goal is. That uncertainty is the specification doing its job: it surfaces the thinking you have not done yet. Do the thinking. Then write the sentence.

props.artifactHeading: The goal the task serves — not the task itself
artifactLines: TASK: 'Summarize this report.'
INTENT: 'Give the editorial desk five reportable-angle bullets for the 9 AM standup — so the desk picks the two stories to commission this week.'
— Not the same. The model cannot know which one you mean if you don't say it.
Write it in one sentence. If you need more: you haven't decided the goal yet.
That uncertainty is the spec doing its job. Do the thinking first.

beats.narration_text: Constraints are what the output must fit inside — format, length, source, audience. Five bullets, one clause each, under a hundred words total, only claims in the document, audience has not read the report. These are things the model would otherwise guess at and guess wrong. Success criteria are how you will know, after the fact, that the output is good — and they must be testable. 'The editor uses it in the standup without rewriting' is a real success criterion. 'It sounds good' is not. Write success criteria as if you were going to hand them to someone who does not know your context and ask them to evaluate whether the output passed. If they cannot evaluate it, the criterion is not testable yet.

props.artifactHeading: Close the guessing gap — then make success checkable
artifactLines: CONSTRAINTS: format (5 bullets, 1 clause each), length (≤100 words), source (report only), audience (hasn't read it).
→ These are things the model would guess at — and guess wrong.
SUCCESS CRITERIA: 'The editor uses it without rewriting.' / 'Each bullet survives: show me where in the report.'
→ Testable means: someone who doesn't know your context could evaluate pass/fail.
'Sounds good' and 'is useful' are not criteria — they are wishes.
Write criteria you could hand to an evaluator and walk away.

beats.narration_text: Exclusions are what the model is not supposed to do — and they must be honest about your role's typical failure modes, not generic. 'Do not hallucinate' is generic. 'Do not editorialize beyond what the report supports; do not present the trade group's framing as if independent; do not pad bullets to feel substantive' — those are exclusions that know the job. The assignment will read generic exclusions immediately. They feel like you wrote them to fill the field. Write the things that would actually go wrong in your domain, the ways a model gets this specific task wrong for a practitioner like you. Output format is simpler: the structural shape of the deliverable — five bullets on their own lines, no preamble, no sub-bullets — because format constrains the kind of thinking the model does, and a different format will produce different content, not just different presentation.

props.artifactHeading: Domain-shaped, not generic — then name the exact structure
artifactLines: EXCLUSIONS — generic (loses points): 'Do not hallucinate.'
EXCLUSIONS — domain-shaped (earns points): 'Do not editorialize beyond the report. Do not present the trade group's framing as if independent. Do not pad.'
→ Write the failure modes that actually happen in your specific task.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Five bullets, one clause each, own lines, no preamble, no sub-bullets.'
→ Format constrains model thinking — different format = different content, not just different presentation.
→ This is not a detail. Specify it.

beats.narration_text: Three traps kill this component. The first: only stating intent. The model gets an intent statement and five components that are really one component rephrased four times. Read your draft: if the constraints, criteria, exclusions, and format all say variations of the same thing your intent already said, they are not doing separate work. The second: success criteria you cannot test. 'The output is high quality' cannot be evaluated by anyone who is not you — which means it evaluates nothing. The third, and the most expensive: writing the spec after the prompt. A spec retrofitted from a finished prompt reads as a justification, not a constraint — because it is. The grader is looking for evidence the spec preceded the build. Spec first. The prompt encodes it.

props.artifactTitle: Three Traps That Kill This Component
props.artifactHeading: All three are common — none are subtle
artifactLines: TRAP 1 — Only intent: constraints/criteria/exclusions all restate intent. Each component must do separate work.
TRAP 2 — Untestable criteria: 'high quality,' 'useful,' 'sounds good.' If an outsider can't evaluate it, it isn't a criterion.
TRAP 3 — Retrofitted spec: you write the prompt first, then justify it with five components.
→ A retrofitted spec reads as a justification. The grader knows the difference.
→ Spec first. Write the five components. Then encode them in the prompt.

beats.narration_text: The deliverable for this episode is the five-component specification — all five present, domain-specific, written before the prompt. Label each component clearly. Put it in its own section. Do not collapse them. If you have done it right, someone reading only the spec — without seeing the prompt — can predict what the prompt must do, what it cannot do, and what a passing output looks like. That is the test: does the spec constrain the prompt from the outside in, or does it describe the prompt from the inside out? The first is a specification. The second is a summary. Write the first.

props.artifactHeading: Five components — all present, domain-specific, written before the prompt
artifactLines: Intent / Constraints / Success Criteria / Exclusions / Output Format — each labeled, each its own section.
Test: can someone reading only the spec predict what the prompt must and cannot do?
If yes: it is a specification — it constrains from the outside in.
If no: it is a summary — it describes from the inside out.
Write the one that constrains.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: 'I am writing the five-component specification for my Botspeak Prompt Adaptation. My role statement is: [your role statement from E1]. My task is: [what the adapted prompt will ask the model to help with]. Draft the five-component specification for this: Intent, Constraints, Success Criteria, Exclusions, Output Format. For each component, flag if it is domain-generic — something that could apply to any similar role — and suggest one replacement that is specific to the failure modes of this exact job.' Read the flags it raises. The domain-generic phrases are the ones that cost points. Fix each one before you write the prompt in episode three.

props.segment: Draft the specification before you write the prompt
props.command: Draft my five-component specification. For each component, flag anything domain-generic and suggest one domain-specific replacement tied to the actual failure modes of this exact role.
props.runningText: paste this into Claude — fix the generic components before episode 3…
output: Intent / Constraints / Success Criteria / Exclusions / Output Format — all five drafted

beats.narration_text: Spec Before You Build: The Five Components. Liam, in for Bear.
